content:
The patient presented with exertional chest discomfort and dyspnea on exertion. Initial electrocardiography showed nonspecific ST-T wave changes, and high-sensitivity troponin was mildly elevated at 0.08 ng/mL. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed preserved left ventricular ejection fraction at 55% with mild left ventricular hypertrophy and normal diastolic function. BNP was modestly elevated at 285 pg/mL. Given the clinical presentation and objective findings suggestive of possible coronary artery disease, the patient was referred for diagnostic cardiac catheterization to further risk-stratify and guide management.

Coronary angiography was performed via right radial access without complication. Findings revealed mild atherosclerotic disease of the left anterior descending artery with approximately 40% stenosis, a patent left circumflex artery, and a right-dominant system with mild disease in the right coronary artery. No acute coronary syndrome was identified, and no intervention was warranted. The patient tolerated the procedure well with stable hemodynamics throughout. Post-procedure angiographic images were reviewed with the patient and family, and the benign findings were discussed in detail.

During the hospital stay, the patient remained hemodynamically stable on her home medications, which included lisinopril and atorvastatin. Troponin levels trended down to baseline. She was discharged on hospital day four in stable condition with outpatient cardiology follow-up arranged. The patient was counseled on lifestyle modifications including cardiac rehabilitation, dietary sodium restriction, and regular aerobic exercise as tolerated. She was instructed to continue her current medications and to report any recurrent chest pain or dyspnea to her primary care physician. Close outpatient follow-up was emphasized given her age and comorbidities.
